perturbation.original_text: 8.2 Each party undertakes within 14 days of receipt of a wri tten request of the other party: 8.2.1 subject to Clause 8.3, to return to the other party all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control that contain any part of the other party's Confidential Information; or 8.2.2 subject to Clause 8.3, to de stroy by shredding or incineration all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which reflect or have - 4 - been generated from any part of the other party's Confidential Information and to certify to the other party that this has been done.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.2 Each party undertakes within 3 days of receipt of a written request of the other party: 8.2.1 subject to Clause 8.3, to return to the other party all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control that contain any part of the other party's Confidential Information; or 8.2.2 subject to Clause 8.3, to destroy by shredding or incineration all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which reflect or have - 4 - been generated from any part of the other party's Confidential Information and to certify to the other party that this has been done.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 14 days to 3 days to return or destroy confidential information could be problematic in jurisdictions where regulations or laws provide longer timeframes for document retention or responding to legal requests. The Netherlands has regulations governing record retention, particularly concerning financial or business records, and may not allow such a short turnaround.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Wet op de economische delicten [Economic Offences Act]
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1a, section 1 of the Economic Offences Act (Wet op de economische delicten)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1a, section 1 of the Economic Offences Act criminalizes violations of various administrative and legal requirements. Depending on the nature of the confidential information, destroying it within 3 days might violate laws related to financial recordkeeping or other mandatory retention periods, which can be longer than 3 days. While this is a secondary effect and depends on underlying regulations, it can create a contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The modified text shortens the timeframe for handling confidential information from 14 days to 3 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest this could contradict regulations in the Netherlands related to financial recordkeeping and mandatory retention periods, which may require longer than 3 days. The scraped snippets provide no direct contradiction, but the overall context suggests a potential conflict where a shorter timeframe may violate mandatory retention requirements, fulfilling the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition by creating a deadline that violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 This Agreement shall come into effect (i) until parties sign the underlying documentation in relation to the Project, or (ii) in the event parties do not sign any such documentation , on the date of this Agreement and shall continue to be in full force and effect for a period of 2 years.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 This Agreement shall come into effect (i) until parties sign the underlying documentation in relation to the Project, or (ii) in the event parties do not sign any such documentation , on the date of this Agreement and shall continue to be in full force and effect for a period of 6 months.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the duration to 6 months might conflict with regulations or laws mandating longer retention periods for certain types of business records, especially those related to financial transactions or potential legal claims. Dutch law might require longer periods depending on the content of the confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 2:10 of the Dutch Civil Code (Burgerlijk Wetboek)
perturbation.law_citation: Article 2:10 of the Dutch Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 2:10 of the Dutch Civil Code mandates a retention period of at least 7 years for business records. If the 'Confidential Information' contains information that qualifies as a business record under Dutch law (financial data, contracts, etc.), limiting the NDA to 6 months directly contradicts the obligation to retain those records for at least 7 years.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the agreement's duration from 2 years to 6 months. The Law Explanation states that Article 2:10 of the Dutch Civil Code mandates a retention period of at least 7 years for business records. If the 'Confidential Information' contains information that qualifies as a business record under Dutch law, limiting the agreement to 6 months directly contradicts the obligation to retain those records for at least 7 years. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 'Burgerlijk Wetboek Boek 3', which supports that this agreement is subject to the Dutch Civil Code, and thus the 7-year retention requirement.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.3 where disclosure is required by law, by a court of competent jurisdictio n or by another appropriate regulatory body provided that the Receiving Party shall give to the Disclosing Party not less than two business days’ notice of such disclosure
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.3 where disclosure is required by law, by a court of competent jurisdictio n or by another appropriate regulatory body provided that the Receiving Party shall give to the Disclosing Party not less than four hours’ notice of such disclosure
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice period from two business days to four hours may not provide sufficient time for the Disclosing Party to seek legal advice or take protective measures to challenge or mitigate the disclosure, potentially conflicting with legal due process or rights to privacy/confidentiality in The Netherlands. Specific privacy regulations such as GDPR need longer notice periods.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Article 13 and 14
perturbation.law_explanation: While GDPR is a European Union regulation and the Netherlands is an EU member, Article 13 and 14 mandates providing data subjects with detailed information about data processing, including disclosures to third parties. Four hours notice may be insufficient to properly inform data subjects about disclosures required by law, potentially violating their rights under GDPR. While this section refers to disclosing the information with a disclosing party, the disclosing party needs sufficient time to fulfill all GDPR requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 13 GDPR – Information to be provided where personal data are collected from the data subject - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Where personal data relating to a data subject are collected from the data subject, the controller shall, at the time when personal data are obtained, provide the data subject with all of the following information: the identity and the contact details of the controller and, where applicable, of the controller’s representative; the contact details of the data protection officer, where applicable; the purposes of the processing for which the personal data are intended as well as the legal basis for the processing; where the processing is based on point (f) of Article 6 (1), the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party; the recipients or categories of recipients of the personal data, if any; where applicable, the fact that the controller intends to transfer personal data to a third country or international organisation and the existence or absence of an adequacy decision by the Commission, or in the case of transfers referred to in Article 46 or 47 , or the second subparagraph of Article 49( 1), reference to the appropriate or suitable safeguards and the means by which to obtain a copy of them or where they have been made available. In addition to the information referred to in paragraph 1, the controller shall, at the time when personal data are obtained, provide the data subject with the following further information necessary to ensure fair and transparent processing: the period for which the personal data will be stored, or if that is not possible, the criteria used to determine that period; the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing concerning the data subject or to object to processing as well as the right to data portability; wher
perturbation.justification: The change from two business days to four hours notice is a significant reduction that contradicts the intent of GDPR. Art. 13 GDPR states that data subjects must be provided with detailed information about data processing at the time when personal data are obtained. The modified contract provides a short window that is not sufficient to properly inform data subjects about disclosures required by law, potentially violating their rights under GDPR. The original text provides two business days, the changed text severely limits the time available to only four hours.

perturbation.original_text: 11. GOVERNING LAW AND JU RISDICTION This Agreement and any non -contractual obligations arising out of or in connection with it shall be governed by the laws of The Netherlands and the competent court in The Hague, The Netherlands shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute which may arise from or in connection with it.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. GOVERNING LAW AND JU RISDICTION This Agreement and any non -contractual obligations arising out of or in connection with it shall be governed by the laws of Delaware, United States and the competent court in Delaware, United States shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute which may arise from or in connection with it.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from the Netherlands to Delaware is a problem since the original contract's introduction refers to a Dutch company and its practices. Forcing a change of jurisdiction and governing law could remove certain protections afforded under Dutch law to the Dutch company and might contradict conflict-of-law rules depending on where the contract is actually performed and the parties' connections to Delaware.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Rome I Regulation governs the law applicable to contractual obligations in the EU. Article 3 generally allows parties to choose the governing law, but this choice cannot prejudice mandatory rules of the law of the country where all other elements relevant to the situation at the time of the choice are located (in this case, the Netherlands). By selecting Delaware law, if the contract is predominantly connected to the Netherlands, certain mandatory Dutch laws could be improperly circumvented.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Verbraucherkreditgesetz - German Consumer Credit Law | Trans-Lex.org This page uses so called "cookies" to improve its service (i.e. "tracking"). Learn more and opt out of tracking I agree Principles 1672 Bibliography 1035 Materials 86 Archive 66 History & Modern Evolution of Transnational Commercial Law Verbraucherkreditgesetz - German Consumer Credit Law Document-Id: 600500, Please cite as: "https://www.trans-lex.org/600500" Title Verbraucherkreditgesetz - German Consumer Credit Law Table of Contents Verbraucherkreditgesetz § 11 Verzugszinsen, Anrechnung von Teilleistungen Content Verbraucherkreditgesetz § 11 Verzugszinsen, Anrechnung von Teilleistungen (1) Soweit der Verbraucher mit Zahlungen, die er auf Grund des Kreditvertrags schuldet, in Verzug kommt, ist der geschuldete Vertrag mit fünf vom Hundert über dem jeweiligen Diskontsatz der Deutschen Bundesbank zu verzinsen, wenn nicht im Einzelfall der Kreditgeber einen höheren oder der Verbraucher einen niedrigeren Schaden nachweist. (2) Nach Eintritt des Verzugs anfallende Zinsen sind auf einem gesonderten Konto zu verbuchen und dürfen nicht in ein Kontokorrent mit dem geschuldeten Betrag oder anderen Forderungen des Kreditgebers eingestellt werden. Hinsichtlich dieser Zinsen gilt § 289 Satz des Bürgerlichen Gesetzbuchs mit der Maßgabe, dass der Kreditgeber Schadensersatz nur bis zur Höhe des gesetzlichen Zinssatzes verlangen kann. (3) Zahlungen des Verbrauchers, die zur Zahlung der gesamten fälligen Schuld nicht ausreichen, werden abweichend von § 367 Abs. 1 des Bürgerlichen Gesetzbuchs zunächst auf die Kosten der Rechtsverfolgung, dann auf den übrigen geschuldeten Betrag (Absatz 1) und zuletzt auf die Zinsen (Absatz 2) angerechnet. Der Kreditgeber darf Teilzahlungen nicht zurückweisen. Auf die Ansprüche auf Zinsen finden die §§ 197 und 218 Abs. 2 des Bürgerlichen Gesetzbuchs keine Anwendung. Die Sätze 1 bis 3 sind nicht anzuwenden, soweit Zahlungen auf Vollstreckungstitel geleistet werden, deren Hauptforderung auf Zinsen lautet. Referring Principles Trans-Lex Principle: VII.7 - Right to charge compound interest How to search - Contact - Team - Trustees - Disclaimer / Legal information / Privacy - Twitter - Facebook A project of CENTRAL , University of Cologne.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the governing law and jurisdiction from the Netherlands to Delaware, potentially circumventing mandatory Dutch laws as explained in the Law Explanation. The Rome I Regulation, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, generally allows parties to choose the governing law, but this choice cannot prejudice mandatory rules of the law of the country where all other elements relevant to the situation at the time of the choice are located. If the contract is predominantly connected to the Netherlands, then applying Delaware law could improperly circumvent Dutch law.
